title: How Should Pre-Trained Language Models Be Fine-Tuned Towards Adversarial Robustness?

structured.summary: The paper proposes Robust Informative Fine-Tuning (RIFT), a novel method for improving adversarial robustness of fine-tuned language models by encouraging retention of pre-trained features through mutual information. It demonstrates improved robustness in selected NLP tasks compared to state-of-the-art methods, although detailed metrics and experimental setups are sparse.

strengths.point: Identification of catastrophic forgetting as a significant problem in adversarial fine-tuning.
Proposes a novel method for fine-tuning pre-trained language models with an information-theoretical approach.
Demonstrates empirical improvements in robustness for sentiment analysis and natural language inference tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of Novelty in Problem Identification
Lack of Numerical Results or Graphs
weaknesses.evidence: General absence of tables or figures
General lack of detail on mutual information computation
General absence of trade-off analysis between pre-trained retention and task-specific adaptation

questions.question: What specific datasets and attack types were used in the experiments?
questions.motivation: Clarification on datasets and attack types used is crucial for assessing the comprehensiveness and validity of the results.

questions.question: How was mutual information practically computed during experiments?
questions.motivation: Understanding the practical computation of mutual information is necessary for replicating the method and validating the claims.

questions.question: Are there trade-offs between retaining pre-trained features and adapting to specific tasks?
questions.motivation: Exploring potential trade-offs can provide insights into the method’s versatility and performance across diverse tasks.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Problem Identification. Lines 28-29 and Line 80
critique_points.point_003: Sparse Experimental Details and Metrics. Lines 12-14
critique_points.point_007: Insufficient Comparison with Baseline Methods. Line 58
critique_points.point_008: Abstract Lacks Clarity on Contributions. Lines 50-56
critique_points.point_009: Potential Over-reliance on Pre-trained Weights. Lines 46-47
critique_points.point_010: Lack of Numerical Results or Graphs. General absence of tables or figures
critique_points.point_011: Ambiguity in Mutual Information Application. General lack of detail on mutual information computation
critique_points.point_012: Exploration of Trade-offs. General absence of trade-off analysis between pre-trained retention and task-specific adaptation
